q2.text: What matters most right now is building a business that creates real financial freedom while maintaining strong relationships with my family. I'm focused on career growth and increasing income, but I also value being present for my kids. Long-term, I want to create a company that runs without me being the bottleneck—something scalable that generates wealth and gives me time back. A meaningful life would mean I built something significant, provided well for my family, and didn't sacrifice relationships to get there. I'm aiming for financial independence by 45, with passive income covering expenses so I can choose projects based on impact rather than necessity.

q11.text: I'm motivated by building things that matter—creating systems that work, seeing measurable results, and knowing I moved the needle. I care deeply about autonomy and hate being micromanaged or stuck in bureaucracy. Recognition matters less than results. I want to see progress, not just talk about it. What frustrates me most is wasted time, unclear direction, and people who talk but don't execute. I'm energized by people who are sharp, direct, and get things done without drama. I get drained by people who need constant hand-holding or who resist accountability.

q17.text: Under pressure, I narrow my focus and execute faster. I stop debating and just make decisions. I move quickly, sometimes before getting full input from others. I tend to push harder on the team and expect them to match my urgency. I know I can become more directive and less patient when things get tight. People have told me I sometimes steamroll discussions when I'm stressed. I also tend to work longer hours and sacrifice balance to hit deadlines. My default is to take control and drive outcomes myself rather than wait for consensus.

q19.text: I communicate in a direct, efficient way. I lead with the point, then provide context if needed. I don't spend a lot of time on pleasantries unless the relationship requires it. I value clarity and speed over politeness. I ask questions to understand quickly, not to build rapport first. In meetings, I prefer structure—agenda, decisions, action items—over open-ended discussions. I'm comfortable challenging ideas and expect the same in return. Some people find me blunt, but I see it as respectful honesty. I adapt my style when I'm dealing with someone who needs more relational warmth, but it costs energy.

q21.text: I tend to set the direction early and then rally people around it. I'm comfortable making decisions without full consensus if time is short. I believe in establishing clear goals, assigning accountability, and then getting out of the way unless someone needs help. I'm not a micromanager, but I do expect updates and results. I think good leadership is about creating clarity and removing obstacles, not holding people's hands. I value autonomy and expect my team to operate with ownership. When someone repeatedly underperforms or resists direction, I address it directly and quickly. I don't let dysfunction linger.

q23.text: I'm energized by fast-paced, results-driven environments where decisions happen quickly and performance is measured clearly. I work best when I have autonomy and am judged by outcomes, not process. I thrive in competitive environments where execution matters more than politics. I struggle in environments that are consensus-driven, slow-moving, or where relationship management is prioritized over results. Bureaucracy and excessive meetings drain me. I need clarity, accountability, and room to move fast.

q24.text: When things stall, my attention goes to execution gaps—who isn't delivering, where systems are breaking, and whether we have the right people in the right roles. My biggest frustration is when I can see the path forward but the team isn't executing at the speed or quality I expect. My bottleneck is usually people—either talent gaps, accountability gaps, or cultural resistance. I'm energized by sharp, driven people who need minimal direction. I'm drained by people who need constant motivation or who avoid hard conversations. When people resist my direction repeatedly, I lose patience fast. I know I should invest more in coaching and development, but I tend to move too quickly and expect people to keep up. What I avoid dealing with: firing people sooner. I wait too long hoping they'll improve. I know I underperform in delegation—I still try to do too much myself because I don't trust others to execute at my standard. The business I'm trying to create is one that scales without me as the bottleneck, but I'm not there yet.

q26.text: I'm in leadership and production. Current yearly revenue is around $2.1M in closed business. My goal for the next 12 months is $3.5M. My database is roughly 800 contacts, but realistically only about 200 would take my call or refer me without hesitation. The rest are cold or stale. I don't have a lead problem right now—I have a conversion and follow-up problem. I'm strong at prospecting and initial engagement but inconsistent at follow-up and nurturing longer sales cycles. My team is five people. Performance is uneven—two are excellent, two are average, one is underperforming and probably needs to go. The biggest gap right now is systems and accountability. We don't have enough structure around pipeline management, and I haven't built strong enough performance tracking. I know we have enough opportunity and talent to hit our goals, but execution discipline is weak.

q27.text: If I had to lead generate today, I'd start by calling five past clients for referrals, then I'd reach out to three centers of influence in my network. After that, I'd probably hit a networking event or cold-call a target list. I feel strongest at prospecting and closing. I'm decent at relationship building but not as consistent as I should be. Follow-up is my weakest area. If you tripled my goals right now, my honest reaction would be: excitement mixed with pressure. I'd immediately start thinking about what systems and people I'd need to add. I'd feel energized by the challenge but also stressed about whether my current infrastructure could handle it. I'd worry about whether I could maintain quality while scaling that fast. But I'd say yes and figure it out.

q28.text: Systems-wise, I use a CRM (HubSpot) but I'm inconsistent with it. I have a rough follow-up process but no formal cadence. My best habit is a weekly pipeline review, but I don't enforce it with the team consistently. Do I believe my systems are strong enough? No. I know they're not. I have a business coach who holds me accountable monthly, and I have a peer group that challenges me. I consider myself coachable in theory, but I resist advice that feels like it will slow me down. I'll listen, but I often default back to my way if I don't immediately see the value. After answering these questions, I'm realizing how much of my bottleneck is me—I say I want to scale, but I'm still operating like a solopreneur. I avoid building the infrastructure because it feels like it will slow me down in the short term, even though I know it's required for long-term growth.
